answer:
In Chapter 5 of *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, Charles Comte defines "natural laws" (lois naturelles) as invariable relations between causes and their effects in human nature—much like physical laws—governing prosperity or decline.[^1] He does not enumerate a fixed or exhaustive list of such laws (and critiques jurisconsults like Montesquieu who propose rigid ones, such as seeking peace, nourishment, reproduction, or society[^2]). Instead, he illustrates the concept through concrete examples of physiological, instinctual, and behavioral necessities that produce inevitable outcomes.[^3]

Comte's key examples include:

- Prolonged abstinence from food causes pain or death.[^4]
- Exposure of the body to fire warms or burns it, depending on proximity.[^4]
- Deprivation of sufficient breathable air causes suffering or death, proportional to duration and extent.[^4]
- Union of the sexes causes multiplication of the species.[^4]
- Pleasures repeated too often or prolonged too long weaken the organs.[^4]
- Moderate exercise strengthens the organs.[^4]

These demonstrate how humans, sharing the same faculties and needs, prosper when needs are met in balance (e.g., via security, nourishment) and decline otherwise (e.g., via fear, scarcity).[^5] Later, Comte reiterates that such laws encompass all "causes of prosperity and causes of decline" inherent to things, whose effects are "infallible" regardless of human knowledge or violation.[^6]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, ch.5 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/#p-2c4dfb).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, ch.5 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/#p-36e87f).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, ch.5 [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/](works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, ch.5 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/#p-2c4dfb).

[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, ch.5 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/#p-f10f47).

[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, ch.5 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/#p-6b78d0).
